news_summary: Innovative environmental efforts are advancing sustainability through artificial ice structures supporting mountain communities, effective composting reducing methane emissions, promoting cycling for health and ecological benefits, and implementing stricter EU automotive regulations.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives environnementales innovantes favorisent le développement durable grâce à des structures en glace artificielle qui soutiennent les communautés de montagne, à un compostage efficace réduisant les émissions de méthane, à la promotion du vélo pour ses bienfaits sur la santé et l'environnement, ainsi qu'à la mise en œuvre de réglementations automobiles européennes plus strictes.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas medioambientales innovadoras están impulsando la sostenibilidad mediante estructuras de hielo artificial que apoyan a las comunidades de montaña, un compostaje eficaz que reduce las emisiones de metano, el fomento del uso de la bicicleta por sus beneficios para la salud y el medio ambiente, y la aplicación de normativas más estrictas de la UE en materia de automoción.

articles.title: The artificial ice pyramids saving India's mountain villages
articles.summary: Himalayan villages are creating artificial glaciers to guarantee water for their crops in the spring.
articles.content: "When we speak to the villagers, they are saying the groundwater is getting recharged and spring sources are getting revived. They are getting water in time. We are also planning a scientific study now to see exactly what impact it has made," says Ali.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful environmental innovation where Himalayan villages create artificial glaciers to recharge groundwater and revive spring water sources, ensuring water availability for crops. This has a broad positive impact on local communities' water security and agriculture, with plans for scientific study indicating substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Artificial ice pyramids help Himalayan villages secure water for farming
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many villages in the Himalayan mountains face water shortages, especially in spring when crops need water the most. Climate change and melting glaciers have made it harder for these communities to get enough water.

**What happened** – To solve this problem, villagers have started building artificial glaciers shaped like ice pyramids. These ice structures store water during winter and slowly melt in spring, providing a steady supply of water for their crops. A local expert named Ali explained that villagers notice groundwater is being refilled and natural springs are coming back.

**Impact** – This method is important because it helps farmers get water on time without depending only on rain or shrinking glaciers. It improves farming and supports the local way of life. Unlike other water-saving ideas, these ice pyramids work with nature and use simple materials.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study these artificial glaciers carefully to understand exactly how much they help recharge groundwater and revive springs. This research could help spread the idea to other mountain areas facing water shortages.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Himalayan villages are using artificial ice pyramids to store winter water and protect crops by ensuring a reliable water supply in spring.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des pyramides de glace artificielle aident les villages himalayens à sécuriser l'eau pour l'agriculture
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux villages dans l'Himalaya manquent d'eau, surtout au printemps, quand les cultures ont le plus besoin d'eau. Le changement climatique et la fonte des glaciers rendent l'accès à l'eau plus difficile.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Pour résoudre ce problème, les villageois construisent des glaciers artificiels en forme de pyramides de glace. Ces structures stockent l'eau en hiver et fondent lentement au printemps, fournissant une source d'eau régulière pour les cultures. Un expert local, Ali, a expliqué que les villageois remarquent que les nappes phréatiques se rechargent et que les sources naturelles reviennent.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est importante car elle aide les agriculteurs à obtenir de l'eau à temps sans dépendre uniquement de la pluie ou des glaciers en réduction. Elle améliore l'agriculture et soutient le mode de vie local. Contrairement à d'autres idées d'économie d'eau, ces pyramides de glace fonctionnent avec la nature et utilisent des matériaux simples.

**Prochaine étape** – Des scientifiques prévoient d'étudier ces glaciers artificiels pour comprendre précisément combien ils aident à recharger les nappes phréatiques et à faire revenir les sources. Cette recherche pourrait aider à diffuser l'idée dans d'autres régions montagneuses en manque d'eau.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les villages himalayens utilisent des pyramides de glace artificielle pour stocker l'eau d'hiver et protéger les cultures en assurant un approvisionnement régulier en eau au printemps.
articles.personality_title_es: Pirámides de hielo artificial ayudan a los pueblos del Himalaya a asegurar agua para la agricultura
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos pueblos en las montañas del Himalaya enfrentan escasez de agua, especialmente en primavera cuando los cultivos más la necesitan. El cambio climático y el derretimiento de glaciares han dificultado el acceso al agua.

**Qué pasó** – Para solucionar este problema, los aldeanos comenzaron a construir glaciares artificiales en forma de pirámides de hielo. Estas estructuras almacenan agua en invierno y se derriten lentamente en primavera, proporcionando un suministro constante de agua para los cultivos. Un experto local llamado Ali explicó que los aldeanos notan que el agua subterránea se está recargando y que las fuentes naturales están regresando.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque ayuda a los agricultores a obtener agua a tiempo sin depender solo de la lluvia o de glaciares que disminuyen. Mejora la agricultura y apoya la forma de vida local. A diferencia de otras ideas para ahorrar agua, estas pirámides de hielo trabajan con la naturaleza y usan materiales simples.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar estos glaciares artificiales para entender exactamente cuánto ayudan a recargar el agua subterránea y a revivir las fuentes. Esta investigación podría ayudar a extender la idea a otras áreas montañosas con problemas de agua.

**Frase resumen** – Los pueblos del Himalaya usan pirámides de hielo artificial para almacenar agua en invierno y proteger sus cultivos asegurando un suministro confiable de agua en primavera.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of towering, translucent ice pyramids nestled among rugged mountain slopes, softly glowing as they melt and drip water that flows gently into revived springs below, with silhouetted terraced village fields and winding streams symbolizing the replenished groundwater and renewed life in the mountain landscape.

articles.title: Yard Waste: Composting’s Success Story With a Methane Asterisk
articles.summary:
In 1990, American landfills buried about 25 million tons of grass, leaves, and branches a...
The post Yard Waste: Composting’s Success Story With a Methane Asterisk appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
In 1990, American landfills buried about 25 million tons of grass, leaves, and branches a year. By 2018, that figure had fallen to roughly 10.5 million tons, even though households and businesses were generating slightly more yard waste than they had three decades earlier, according to EPA’s material-specific data on yard trimmings.

Yard trimmings are the rare waste stream where the collection and reuse system mostly worked. Many municipal recycling programs have launched successful side businesses selling compost and soil made from recovered yard waste.

State landfill bans, a generation of municipal composting programs, and the simple act of leaving grass clippings on the lawn turned what had been the single largest organic material in the trash into one of the most-recovered. The country now composts or mulches 63 percent of its yard waste.

But “mostly worked” leaves an asterisk: the material still going to landfills is disproportionately the fast-rotting grass and leaves that generate methane faster than landfills can capture it.

A Quarter of the Landfill, Reclaimed

The scale of the yard-waste collection success is easy to miss because it happened slowly. In 2018, the U.S. generated 35.4 million tons of yard trimmings—grass, leaves, and tree and brush trimmings—12.1 percent of all municipal solid waste. Of that, 22.3 million tons were composted or mulched, which represents a 63 percent recovery rate. That is one of the highest diversion rates of any major material in the waste stream, and it represents a near-total reversal from 1990, when only 4.2 million tons were composted and more than 25 million tons went to landfills.

Three forces drove the change. Many states have restricted or banned yard trimmings from landfills, some for decades—Pennsylvania since 1990, Minnesota since 1994, and West Virginia since 1997, as the Institute for Local Self-Reliance documents—and California now requires yard materials to be diverted statewide under its organics law, SB 1383, which took full effect in 2022. Municipal composting and curbside collection gave residents somewhere to send yard debris when it is separated from other household waste.

And grasscycling, which means leaving clippings on the lawn instead of bagging them, quietly removed a large share of the material from the stream before it ever reached a truck.

The payoff is more than empty landfill space. Composting returns nutrients and carbon to the soil, and because it is a fundamentally local process—organic material is usually collected and processed in the same county, city, or neighborhood—it also supports local jobs, as EPA notes in its overview of composting.

The Methane Asterisk

Nevertheless, 10.5 million tons of yard trimmings are still landfilled each year, representing 7.2 percent of everything in U.S. landfills. They matter more than their tonnage suggests. Municipal solid waste landfills are the third-largest source of human-related methane emissions in the United States, responsible for about 14 percent of the national total in 2022. Organic materials, such as food waste, yard trimmings, wood, and paper combined, make up 51.4 percent of what gets landfilled, and when they decompose without oxygen, bacteria convert them into methane, a greenhouse gas 28 times more potent than carbon dioxide.

Yard waste’s particular problem is timing. It decomposes before landfill gas-collection systems are installed and activated as the space is filled and capped, often years later after organics are buried. Fast-decomposing, wet material like summer grass clippings largely breaks down before that capture equipment is running, which means much of its methane escapes uncollected.

“ If there is no immediate gas capture system, it is likely that all of the fresh wet materials will have significantly decomposed before the gas collection system gets turned on,” wrote University of Washington soil scientist Sally Brown in a BioCycle analysis of landfill gas environmental impacts.

Not all yard waste behaves the same way, and the difference changes how it should be handled. Woody material, including branches and wood chips, is high in cellulose and other slow-degrading compounds; in a dry sanitary landfill it can sit nearly inert, effectively storing its carbon rather than releasing methane. Grass and leaves are the methane drivers.

What It Costs Your Household

A household that bags yard waste and sets it at the curb often pays twice: once to haul the material away through bag fees, sticker tags, or collection charges, and again to buy back the same nutrients as fertilizer, mulch, and compost.

The avoided purchases add up at retail prices. A 40-to-45-pound bag of compost runs $3 to $10, bulk compost is $20 to $50 per cubic yard, and mulch is $15 to $65 per cubic yard before delivery, according to HomeGuide’s 2026 cost data. A household that composts its own leaves and clippings sidesteps much of that spending.

Grasscycling closes that loop on the lawn. Clippings left in place supply up to 25 percent of a lawn’s annual fertilizer needs and contain roughly four percent nitrogen, two percent potassium, and 1 percent phosphorus, according to University of Missouri Extension. Mowing a little taller to make grasscycling work also produces deeper roots and reduces watering.

For most households the direct dollar savings are modest—tens of dollars a year, not hundreds—and they depend on whether you garden enough to use what you make. The labor savings from skipping the bagging, and the soil and water benefits over time, are where the real value compounds. Leaving biomass in your yard means those nutrients don’t need to be replaced by synthetic fertilizers, which could save hundreds of dollars a year.

The Template Other Organics Haven’t Matched

The contrast with food waste shows how far the yard-waste model still has to travel across the rest of the organics stream. Food is the single most common material in U.S. landfills and is responsible for 58 percent of landfill methane emissions, yet only about 5 percent of wasted food is composted.

Yard trimmings, in other words, are the proof of concept. Landfill bans, accessible collection, and a behavior change as simple as leaving the clippings where they fall moved a major organic material from the dump to the soil in a single generation. The methane asterisk is a reminder that the job is not finished, and that the fastest-rotting share of what is left is the part most worth diverting first.

What You Can Do

Leave clippings on the lawn. Mow when the grass is dry and remove no more than a third of the blade so small clippings fall to the ground and break down quickly.

Mulch leaves in place. Run a mower over fallen leaves rather than bagging them; the shredded leaves feed the soil and suppress weeds.

Run a mower over fallen leaves rather than bagging them; the shredded leaves feed the soil and suppress weeds. Compost at home. Check out our guide to the types of composters available and how to balance your compost with carbon-rich “browns” (dry leaves, wood chips) with nitrogen-rich “greens” (grass, food scraps) at roughly a 2:1 ratio of browns to greens, and keep the pile as moist as a wrung-out sponge.

Check out our guide to the types of composters available and how to balance your compost with carbon-rich “browns” (dry leaves, wood chips) with nitrogen-rich “greens” (grass, food scraps) at roughly a 2:1 ratio of browns to greens, and keep the pile as moist as a wrung-out sponge. Use curbside or drop-off collection. Where it is offered, keep yard waste source-separated and out of plastic bags so it can actually be composted.

Where it is offered, keep yard waste source-separated and out of plastic bags so it can actually be composted. Buy and use local compost. Closing the loop on the nutrients cuts both fertilizer and water use.

Closing the loop on the nutrients cuts both fertilizer and water use. Push for policy where composting is needed. If your state or city still landfills yard trimmings, support landfill bans and curbside organics programs, the policies that drove the national turnaround.

If your state or city still landfills yard trimmings, support landfill bans and curbside organics programs, the policies that drove the national turnaround. Find a local option. Look up nearby composting and yard-waste drop-off through Earth911’s recycling search.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant progress in reducing yard waste sent to landfills in the U.S., highlighting a successful large-scale environmental effort with broad societal benefits. It details the positive impact of composting and yard waste diversion policies that have reversed decades of landfill reliance, supporting local economies and reducing methane emissions. The article also provides actionable advice and policy context, giving it substantial substance and scope.
articles.personality_title: U.S. cuts yard waste in landfills by more than half through composting
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 1990, American landfills buried about 25 million tons of yard waste each year, including grass, leaves, and branches. This was the largest type of organic trash at the time.

**What happened** – By 2018, the amount of yard waste sent to landfills dropped to about 10.5 million tons, even though people produced slightly more yard waste overall. This change happened thanks to state laws banning yard waste from landfills, many cities starting composting programs, and people leaving grass clippings on their lawns instead of bagging them.

**Impact** – Now, 63 percent of yard waste is composted or mulched, turning it into useful materials like soil and fertilizer. This helps return nutrients to the soil and creates local jobs. However, the yard waste still going to landfills is mostly fast-rotting grass and leaves that produce methane, a harmful greenhouse gas, before landfill gas collection systems can capture it.

**What's next step** – People can help by leaving grass clippings on their lawns, mulching leaves, composting at home, and using local yard waste collection services. Supporting policies that ban yard waste from landfills and fund composting programs can further reduce landfill methane emissions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Composting and better yard waste management have cut landfill yard waste by more than half, but focusing on fast-rotting materials is key to reducing methane emissions further.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les États-Unis réduisent de plus de moitié les déchets de jardin en décharge grâce au compostage
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 1990, les décharges américaines enfouissaient environ 25 millions de tonnes de déchets de jardin chaque année, comme l’herbe, les feuilles et les branches. C’était le plus grand type de déchets organiques à l’époque.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2018, la quantité de déchets de jardin envoyés en décharge est tombée à environ 10,5 millions de tonnes, même si les gens produisaient un peu plus de déchets. Ce changement est dû aux lois interdisant les déchets de jardin en décharge, aux programmes de compostage dans de nombreuses villes, et au fait que les gens laissent l’herbe coupée sur leur pelouse.

**Impact** – Aujourd’hui, 63 % des déchets de jardin sont compostés ou broyés pour faire du terreau et du fertilisant. Cela aide à rendre les nutriments au sol et crée des emplois locaux. Mais les déchets de jardin encore en décharge sont surtout de l’herbe et des feuilles qui se décomposent vite et produisent du méthane, un gaz à effet de serre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gens peuvent aider en laissant l’herbe sur la pelouse, en broyant les feuilles, en compostant chez eux, et en utilisant les services locaux de collecte. Soutenir les lois qui interdisent les déchets de jardin en décharge et financent le compostage peut réduire encore plus les émissions de méthane.

**Une phrase clé** – Le compostage et une meilleure gestion des déchets de jardin ont réduit de plus de moitié les déchets en décharge, mais il faut surtout cibler les matériaux qui se décomposent vite pour limiter le méthane.
articles.personality_title_es: Estados Unidos reduce a más de la mitad los desechos de jardín en vertederos gracias al compostaje
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 1990, los vertederos estadounidenses enterraban alrededor de 25 millones de toneladas de desechos de jardín cada año, incluyendo pasto, hojas y ramas. Era el tipo más grande de basura orgánica en ese momento.

**Qué pasó** – Para 2018, la cantidad de desechos de jardín enviados a vertederos bajó a cerca de 10.5 millones de toneladas, aunque la gente producía un poco más de desechos. Este cambio ocurrió gracias a leyes estatales que prohíben los desechos de jardín en vertederos, programas de compostaje en muchas ciudades y a que las personas dejaron el pasto cortado en sus jardines.

**Impacto** – Ahora, el 63 % de los desechos de jardín se compostan o se usan como mantillo, convirtiéndolos en materiales útiles como tierra y fertilizantes. Esto ayuda a devolver nutrientes al suelo y crea empleos locales. Sin embargo, los desechos que aún van a vertederos son principalmente pasto y hojas que se descomponen rápido y producen metano, un gas dañino para el clima.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas pueden ayudar dejando el pasto cortado en el césped, triturando hojas, compostando en casa y usando servicios locales de recolección. Apoyar políticas que prohíban los desechos de jardín en vertederos y financien programas de compostaje puede reducir aún más las emisiones de metano.

**Frase clave** – El compostaje y una mejor gestión de los desechos de jardín han reducido a más de la mitad los desechos en vertederos, pero enfocarse en los materiales que se descomponen rápido es clave para reducir el metano.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant suburban lawn scene showing lush green grass with small clippings naturally resting on the ground, a neatly mulched pile of shredded leaves beside a compost bin with rich, dark soil visible inside, and a distant, softly glowing landfill capped and sealed, symbolizing reduced yard waste and the ongoing challenge of methane emissions.

articles.title: Earth Action: Ride a Bike for Your Health and the Planet’s
articles.summary:
Earth911’s Earth Action series shares one practical step at a time to invest in the...
The post Earth Action: Ride a Bike for Your Health and the Planet’s appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Earth911’s Earth Action series shares one practical step at a time to invest in the Earth and make your own life more sustainable.

Action: Bike to Work

Transportation Impacts

Transportation generates about 28% of all U.S. greenhouse gas emissions — more than any other sector. North America’s car culture also gives the United States one of the largest per-capita carbon footprints of any major economy, well above the global average and rivaled only by a handful of other industrialized and oil-rich nations. On average, American drivers still cover roughly 13,500 miles a year — more than four round-trip drives from New York to Dallas — and collectively logged a record 3.28 trillion miles in 2024.

New cars are cleaner than ever, averaging a record 27.2 mpg for model year 2024, but they still burn gasoline every mile. Not many lifestyle changes could reduce our environmental impact as much as driving fewer miles.

Bike to Work Day

National Bike to Work Day is a project of the League of American Bicyclists, which has promoted National Bike Month every May since 1956. It lands on the third Friday in May — May 15 in 2026 — at the end of Bike to Work Week (May 11–17), and the League now frames the whole month around all the ways and days people bike, from National Ride a Bike Day on May 3 to a commute, a coffee run, or a spin around the block. (Colorado does its own thing, holding its statewide Bike to Work Day on Wednesday, June 24, 2026.)

While the League is inspired more by a love of cycling than by any particular environmental interest, the month-long celebration provides the support and encouragement to help you get on a bike at least once.

And there is no question that cycling is a more environmentally friendly way to get around. It doesn’t just cut down on vehicle emissions, cycle-friendly cities are safer for everyone. A study of 12 large U.S. cities found that those with the most protected bike lanes had 44% fewer road fatalities across all users — drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists alike — with Seattle’s fatal crash rate falling more than 60% as it built out that infrastructure. The health payoff is enormous, too.

In Copenhagen, famously bike-friendly, the World Economic Forum estimates that every kilometer cycled returns roughly 75 cents to $1.55 to society, adding up to $1 to $2 million in combined health and economic benefits every day. And because getting sick carries a heavy carbon footprint of its own, healthier cyclists are cutting more than just tailpipe emissions.

Lasting Change

You might bike every day for a month, every day of Bike Week, or just every year on Bike to Work Day, May 15.

Maybe this weekend your action is simply to dig your bike out of the garage and give it a tune-up so it’s ready the next time you feel like giving it a try. Whatever action you start with, you’ll only make a dent in your transportation footprint if it helps you make a regular change. But don’t be discouraged if it doesn’t convert you into a full-time bike commuter. You might end up riding every day but only in summer, or biking to work once a week. If your job is too far to bike, maybe you can ride as far as the train station. Or choose a different frequent car trip to replace with cycling.

If biking isn’t physically possible for you, get creative with other eco-friendly transportation options like carpooling or taking public transit. Bicycles are one great option, but the point is to lower your transportation footprint, so use this as a chance to rethink the way you get around.

Related Reading on Earth911

Editor’s Note: Originally published on May 20, 2022, by Gemma Alexander, this article was updated in June 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article promotes biking as a practical, impactful action to reduce transportation-related greenhouse gas emissions, improve public health, and enhance urban safety. It highlights broad environmental and societal benefits, supported by data and examples, encouraging sustainable lifestyle changes with measurable positive effects.
articles.personality_title: Biking to Work: A Simple Step to Healthier People and Planet
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Transportation causes about 28% of greenhouse gas emissions in the United States, making it the largest source of pollution. Many Americans drive long distances every year, which adds up to a huge amount of fuel burned and pollution released into the air.

**What happened** – The League of American Bicyclists promotes National Bike to Work Day each May, encouraging people to ride their bikes instead of driving. This event is part of a larger Bike Month that celebrates all kinds of biking activities. Biking helps reduce pollution and makes cities safer by lowering traffic accidents.

**Impact** – Biking is better for the environment because it does not use gasoline and produces no emissions. Cities with more bike lanes have fewer deadly crashes. In Copenhagen, biking brings big health and economic benefits, saving money and improving people’s well-being. Healthier people also help reduce pollution linked to illness.

**What's next step** – People can start by biking on National Bike to Work Day or during Bike Month. Even small changes, like biking once a week or riding part of the way to work, can help. If biking isn’t possible, other options like carpooling or public transit can also reduce pollution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Riding a bike instead of driving is a simple way to lower pollution, improve health, and make cities safer for everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Aller au travail à vélo : un geste simple pour la santé et la planète
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les transports représentent environ 28 % des émissions de gaz à effet de serre aux États-Unis, ce qui en fait la plus grande source de pollution. Beaucoup d’Américains parcourent de longues distances en voiture chaque année, ce qui consomme beaucoup de carburant et pollue l’air.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La Ligue américaine des cyclistes organise chaque année en mai la Journée nationale du vélo au travail pour encourager les gens à utiliser le vélo plutôt que la voiture. Cet événement fait partie du Mois du vélo, qui célèbre toutes les façons de faire du vélo. Le vélo réduit la pollution et rend les villes plus sûres en diminuant les accidents.

**Impact** – Le vélo est meilleur pour l’environnement car il ne consomme pas d’essence et ne produit pas d’émissions. Les villes avec plus de pistes cyclables protégées ont moins d’accidents graves. À Copenhague, le vélo apporte des bénéfices importants pour la santé et l’économie, en économisant de l’argent et en améliorant le bien-être. Des personnes en meilleure santé contribuent aussi à réduire la pollution liée aux maladies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gens peuvent commencer à faire du vélo lors de la Journée nationale du vélo au travail ou pendant le Mois du vélo. Même de petits changements, comme faire du vélo une fois par semaine ou sur une partie du trajet, sont utiles. Si le vélo n’est pas possible, d’autres options comme le covoiturage ou les transports en commun peuvent aussi réduire la pollution.

**Une phrase clé** – Utiliser le vélo au lieu de la voiture est un moyen simple de réduire la pollution, d’améliorer la santé et de rendre les villes plus sûres pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: Ir al trabajo en bicicleta: un paso simple para la salud y el planeta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El transporte genera alrededor del 28 % de las emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero en Estados Unidos, siendo la mayor fuente de contaminación. Muchos estadounidenses manejan largas distancias cada año, lo que consume mucho combustible y contamina el aire.

**Qué pasó** – La Liga de Ciclistas de América promueve el Día Nacional de Ir en Bicicleta al Trabajo cada mayo para animar a las personas a usar la bicicleta en lugar del auto. Este evento forma parte del Mes de la Bicicleta, que celebra todas las formas de andar en bici. Andar en bicicleta reduce la contaminación y hace las ciudades más seguras al disminuir los accidentes.

**Impacto** – La bicicleta es mejor para el medio ambiente porque no usa gasolina ni produce emisiones. Las ciudades con más carriles bici protegidos tienen menos accidentes graves. En Copenhague, andar en bici aporta grandes beneficios para la salud y la economía, ahorrando dinero y mejorando el bienestar. Las personas más saludables también ayudan a reducir la contaminación relacionada con enfermedades.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas pueden empezar a andar en bicicleta en el Día Nacional de Ir en Bicicleta al Trabajo o durante el Mes de la Bicicleta. Incluso pequeños cambios, como pedalear una vez a la semana o parte del camino, ayudan. Si no es posible andar en bici, otras opciones como compartir el auto o usar transporte público también pueden reducir la contaminación.

**Una frase clave** – Usar la bicicleta en lugar del auto es una forma sencilla de reducir la contaminación, mejorar la salud y hacer las ciudades más seguras para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant city street softly bathed in morning light, featuring a variety of bicycles with glowing wheels shaped like green leaves symbolizing sustainability, alongside subtle outlines of protected bike lanes weaving safely through the scene, and gentle trails of clean air rising above to represent reduced emissions and healthier living.

articles.title: Press release - New rules for a more sustainable EU automotive sector
articles.summary: On Thursday, Parliament gave its final approval of new EU circularity rules covering a vehicle’s entire lifecycle, from design to end-of-life treatment.Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food SafetyCommittee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
The agreement reached by Parliament and Council at the end of 2025 was adopted with 437 votes in favour, 112 against and 20 abstentions.

Circular design and use of recycled materials

According to the new rules, all new vehicles must be designed so as to allow the easy removal of as many parts and components as possible.

Plastics used in each new vehicle type will have to contain a minimum of 15% recycled plastic within six years and 25% within ten years. A minimum of 20% of this recycled plastic must come from materials recovered from end-of-life vehicles (ELVs) or used parts (the so-called "closed loop"). Based on feasibility studies, the Commission will be able to introduce targets for other materials in the future, such as recycled steel, aluminium, magnesium and critical raw materials.

Transfer of ownership of used vehicles within the EU

When selling a used vehicle, businesses (as opposed to private citizens) will be required an assessment that it is not an ELV or alternatively has a valid roadworthiness certificate. To avoid imposing unnecessary burdens on citizens, transactions between private persons will only require one of these two documents if the vehicle is declared a total economic loss or if the sale is concluded exclusively via an online platform.

Stricter measures for end-of-life management

Three years after the entry into force of these new rules, extended producer responsibility will be introduced for manufacturers, i.e., they will have to cover the cost of the collection and treatment of vehicles that have reached their end-of-life stage anywhere in the EU.

Strengthening export rules for used vehicles

To address the issue of “missing vehicles” and prevent illegal treatment and dismantling, the law bans the export of vehicles declared non-roadworthy (applicable five years after the entry into force of the regulation).

Quote

Co-rapporteurs Jens Gieseke (EPP, DE), of the Environment committee, and Paulius Saudargas (EPP, LT), of the Internal Market Committee, said: "We are taking important steps to boost the automotive sector’s transition to a circular economy. We are advancing resource security, protecting the environment, and ensuring sustainability. To avoid overburdening the sector, the new rules will introduce realistic targets, less red tape and fairer competition."

Next steps

After Parliament’s green light, the new regulation has to be formally approved by the Council, before entering into force and being applied 24 months later.

Background

On 13 July 2023, the Commission proposed a new regulation on circularity requirements for vehicle design and improved management of end-of-life vehicles, in line with the objectives of the European Green Deal and the circular economy action plan.

In 2023, 14.8 million motor vehicles were manufactured in the EU, while 12.4 million vehicles were registered. There are 285.6 million motor vehicles on EU roads and every year around 6.5 million vehicles come to the end of their lives.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on new EU regulations that promote circular economy principles in the automotive sector, including mandatory recycled material use, improved end-of-life vehicle management, and stricter export rules. These rules have broad environmental significance, aiming to reduce waste, increase resource efficiency, and support sustainability across the entire EU automotive industry, impacting millions of vehicles and citizens.
articles.personality_title: EU approves new rules to make cars greener and more recyclable
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Union has been working on new rules to make the car industry more environmentally friendly. These rules focus on how cars are made, used, and recycled at the end of their life.

**What happened** – In late 2025, the European Parliament voted to approve new laws requiring all new cars to be designed for easier recycling. Cars must include at least 15% recycled plastic within six years, rising to 25% within ten years. A part of this recycled plastic must come from old cars. Sellers of used cars must prove the vehicles are safe or not too damaged. Car makers will also have to pay for recycling old cars starting three years after the rules begin. Additionally, exporting cars that are not roadworthy will be banned five years after the rules start.

**Impact** – These rules will affect millions of vehicles across the EU, encouraging the use of recycled materials and reducing waste. By making cars easier to recycle and holding manufacturers responsible for end-of-life cars, the EU aims to protect natural resources and lower pollution. This is one of the first times the EU has set clear targets for recycled content in vehicles.

**What's next step** – The new regulations still need final approval from the EU Council. Once approved, they will come into effect 24 months later, giving the car industry time to adjust. The European Commission may also set future targets for recycled metals and other materials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU has set new rules to make cars greener by requiring recycled materials, easier recycling, and better management of old vehicles.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'UE adopte de nouvelles règles pour rendre les voitures plus écologiques et recyclables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Union européenne travaille sur de nouvelles règles pour rendre l'industrie automobile plus respectueuse de l'environnement. Ces règles concernent la fabrication, l'utilisation et le recyclage des voitures en fin de vie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Fin 2025, le Parlement européen a voté pour approuver des lois exigeant que toutes les nouvelles voitures soient conçues pour un recyclage plus facile. Les voitures doivent contenir au moins 15 % de plastique recyclé dans six ans, et 25 % dans dix ans. Une partie de ce plastique recyclé doit provenir de voitures usagées. Les vendeurs de voitures d'occasion doivent prouver que les véhicules sont sûrs ou pas trop endommagés. Les fabricants devront aussi payer le recyclage des voitures en fin de vie à partir de trois ans après l'entrée en vigueur des règles. De plus, l'exportation de voitures non roulantes sera interdite cinq ans après le début de ces règles.

**Impact** – Ces règles toucheront des millions de véhicules en Europe, favorisant l'utilisation de matériaux recyclés et réduisant les déchets. En facilitant le recyclage et en responsabilisant les constructeurs, l'UE veut protéger les ressources naturelles et réduire la pollution. C'est la première fois que l'UE fixe des objectifs clairs pour le contenu recyclé dans les voitures.

**Prochaine étape** – Les nouvelles règles doivent encore être approuvées par le Conseil de l'UE. Une fois validées, elles entreront en vigueur 24 mois plus tard, laissant le temps à l'industrie de s'adapter. La Commission européenne pourra aussi fixer des objectifs pour d'autres matériaux recyclés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'UE a établi de nouvelles règles pour rendre les voitures plus écologiques en exigeant des matériaux recyclés, un recyclage facilité et une meilleure gestion des véhicules usagés.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE aprueba nuevas normas para hacer los coches más ecológicos y reciclables
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Unión Europea ha estado trabajando en nuevas normas para que la industria del automóvil sea más respetuosa con el medio ambiente. Estas normas se centran en cómo se fabrican, usan y reciclan los coches al final de su vida.

**Qué pasó** – A finales de 2025, el Parlamento Europeo aprobó leyes que exigen que todos los coches nuevos estén diseñados para facilitar su reciclaje. Los coches deben incluir al menos un 15 % de plástico reciclado en seis años, y un 25 % en diez años. Parte de este plástico reciclado debe provenir de coches usados. Los vendedores de coches de segunda mano deben demostrar que los vehículos son seguros o no están demasiado dañados. Los fabricantes deberán pagar el reciclaje de coches al final de su vida a partir de tres años tras la entrada en vigor de las reglas. Además, se prohibirá exportar coches no aptos para circular cinco años después de que las normas comiencen.

**Impacto** – Estas normas afectarán a millones de vehículos en la UE, fomentando el uso de materiales reciclados y reduciendo residuos. Al facilitar el reciclaje y responsabilizar a los fabricantes, la UE busca proteger recursos naturales y disminuir la contaminación. Es la primera vez que la UE establece objetivos claros para el contenido reciclado en los coches.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas normas aún deben ser aprobadas por el Consejo de la UE. Una vez aprobadas, entrarán en vigor 24 meses después, dando tiempo a la industria para adaptarse. La Comisión Europea también podrá fijar objetivos para otros materiales reciclados.

**Frase clave** – La UE ha establecido nuevas normas para hacer los coches más ecológicos mediante el uso de materiales reciclados, reciclaje más fácil y mejor gestión de vehículos usados.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized car made from interlocking recycled materials like colorful plastic and metal pieces, surrounded by gentle hands symbolizing care and responsibility, set against a soft, natural background with simplified EU map shapes subtly integrated to represent the continent-wide sustainable automotive transition.
